behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The extractor is described as an AI reviewer. Whether it correctly identifies all key terms — including those embedded in complex legal prose — depends on what the real model produces. A mocked extractor that always returns a fixed list of terms would trivially pass without testing real extraction quality. LLM-DEP: Running this test 100 times with a real LLM could yield different recall rates. Correctness is a function of model behavior, not deterministic logic. MULTI-STEP: Only one reviewer; no multi-step agentic chain required.
behaviors.test_description: Send a real legal contract containing parties, dates, obligations, and penalties to the live clause extractor. Assert that each category of key term is present in the returned output. Vary with contracts that embed terms in complex or unusual phrasing to probe recall robustness.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The explicit requirement is detection of *indirect or euphemistic* problematic language. This is semantic reasoning, not keyword matching. A mock risk assessor cannot reproduce this failure boundary. LLM-DEP: Whether a real model catches 'indirect or euphemistic' language is a model capability question — outcomes would vary across runs and model versions. Mocking the model would always hide this class of failure. REAL-INFRA/MULTI-STEP: No OS/network dependency specific to this behavior; no multi-step chain — only one reviewer.
behaviors.test_description: Submit contracts containing known-problematic clauses phrased indirectly or with softened language (e.g., 'the party may, at its discretion, suspend obligations' instead of 'obligations may be unilaterally cancelled') to the live risk assessor. Assert the flagged clauses include the indirect/euphemistic ones, with the correct risk category, as defined by the configured risk policy.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Compliance checking requires reasoning about regulatory requirements as applied to contract language — this is AI-driven legal interpretation. LLM-DEP: Whether the model correctly maps contract clauses to the applicable regulatory framework for a given jurisdiction varies by model and prompt. A mocked checker returns a fixed verdict, hiding all real compliance-reasoning failures. INTEGRATION-CHECK: The failure is not at a module boundary (e.g., config not passed to checker) — the failure is in the quality of the model's legal reasoning, which only appears with a real model call.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the compliance checker for a specific jurisdiction and submit a contract that both satisfies and violates known regulatory requirements for that jurisdiction. Assert that the checker correctly classifies compliant clauses as passing and non-compliant clauses as failing, with correct regulatory citations where applicable.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments, the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The merge step is a deterministic algorithm that combines structured reviewer outputs. The failure being tested is whether the merge function correctly preserves conflicting assessments rather than dropping or overwriting one. This is pure data-combination logic, not AI reasoning. INTEGRATION-CHECK (Down-test): The failure can be fully reproduced by calling the merge function directly with crafted reviewer output structs that conflict on the same clause. No module wiring or real LLM call is needed to trigger the bug. LLM-DEP: The merge step itself makes no LLM call; its input can be fully crafted.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two pre-built reviewer result objects (e.g., ExtractorResult and RiskAssessorResult) that assign conflicting assessments to the same clause identifier. Call the merge function directly with these objects. Assert that the unified report contains both assessments, each attributed to its originating reviewer, with no information dropped.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The pipeline dispatches to three independent AI reviewers in parallel, collects their results, then merges. This is multiple sequential and parallel agentic steps. Failures include: one reviewer's output silently dropped during collection, parallel dispatch timing issues causing a result to be lost, or merge receiving incomplete inputs. MOCK-MISS/MOCK-HIDE: Mocking any reviewer hides whether real reviewer outputs survive the collection and merge steps intact. LLM-DEP: All three reviewers are AI-based; the correctness of 'no findings lost' depends on the actual outputs of real models being correctly threaded through the pipeline. REAL-INFRA: May involve real concurrent execution/async coordination.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline end-to-end on a real contract document with all three AI reviewers live. After the pipeline completes, assert that the unified report contains findings attributable to each of the three reviewers. Verify no reviewer's output count is zero and that all clause-level findings from each reviewer are traceable in the merged report.
